Q1: I organized my study routine around my work schedule and family responsibilities, which at 29 means I have more on my plate than traditional students. I typically dedicated about 19 hours per week to studying, mostly in the evenings after everything else was settled. I would block out specific times and treat them like appointments I couldn't miss. I relied heavily on written materials and notes since that's how I learn best, so I'd print readings when I could afford the ink and paper.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular because I've learned that consistency is the only way I can manage everything. However, I'd definitely intensify my efforts when deadlines approached. I tried to stay ahead, but sometimes life would throw curveballs and I'd have to adjust. The key for me was having a baseline routine that I could flex when needed rather than starting from scratch each week.
Q3: I usually logged in when I needed to check assignment requirements or submit work, and also to participate in discussions since those were important for my learning. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any announcements or updates from instructors. The peer support connections I made through the course also prompted me to check in on discussion threads to see what others were saying.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine during my commute or before starting my day. I'd quickly scan for any new posts or deadlines while having my tea. It took a few weeks to build this habit, but once I connected with peers in the discussion forums, I found myself wanting to check in more regularly. The social aspect made it feel less like a chore and more like checking in with study partners.
Q5: The discussion forums were incredibly valuable to me because connecting with other students helped me understand concepts from different perspectives. The reading materials and written resources were essential since I'm a read-write learner who processes information best through text. I also appreciated the assignment feedback because it helped me improve incrementally throughout the course. The peer interactions, all 43 of them, really kept me motivated when things got tough.
Q6: I sometimes skipped the video lectures initially because watching nearly 295 minutes of video felt overwhelming with my limited time. I'd try to find transcripts or summaries instead since reading is faster for me. Some supplementary resources that weren't directly tied to assignments got pushed aside because I had to prioritize what would impact my grades most. With limited resources, I couldn't afford to spend time on things that felt optional.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams, or when it helped me understand something I was struggling with. Discussions with peers made activities feel valuable because I wasn't just passively consuming content. If I could apply what I learned immediately or if it filled a gap in my understanding, I'd invest the time. The sense of progress and connection to my goals made the difference.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because I had to weigh them against my limited time and energy. Required activities got done no matter what since my 95% assignment completion rate was non-negotiable for me. Optional ones I'd only tackle if I genuinely felt they'd help me or if I had extra time, which was rare. Sometimes I felt guilty skipping optional content, but I had to be strategic about where I invested my effort.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline because it was more practical for my situation. I'd download readings and print them when possible, then study on the bus or during lunch breaks at work. I also used my own notebooks extensively, rewriting key concepts in my own words since that's how I retain information best. External resources like library books and free online articles supplemented what the course provided.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours reviewing printed notes, creating handwritten summaries, and testing myself with flashcards I made. Study sessions with classmates I'd connected with through the peer support program happened over messaging apps, not on the LMS. My most productive learning often happened away from any screen.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a reading or resource once on the LMS, download or print it, and then work with it offline multiple times over several days. This was partly practical since my internet access wasn't always reliable, and partly because I concentrate better with physical materials. A single download might represent hours of subsequent offline study.
Q12: There were moments when financial stress made it harder to focus on coursework because worrying about bills takes mental energy. Sometimes juggling work and studies left me exhausted, and even though my stress levels were generally manageable, those peaks were challenging. During those times, I leaned more heavily on my peer support network to stay motivated. Having people who understood the struggle made a real difference in pushing through.
Q13: Definitely, when work got demanding or I felt mentally drained, my LMS usage would drop even if my actual studying continued offline. Confusion about certain topics sometimes made me hesitant to engage with new material until I'd sorted out the basics on my own. I'd compensate by being very strategic about when I logged in, making sure each session was purposeful. Competing responsibilities meant I couldn't afford to waste time browsing aimlessly.
Q14: There were times when I'd open materials but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to absorb anything. I'd scroll through readings without really processing them, then have to return later when I could actually focus. Sometimes I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't retained anything because I was multitasking. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they showed up as engagement in the system.
Q15: I occasionally postponed discussion posts when I felt I didn't have anything valuable to contribute yet. Sometimes I'd delay starting complex readings because I wanted to tackle them when I had proper mental energy, not just squeeze them in. I knew these activities were important, but doing them poorly felt worse than doing them a bit late. My approach was quality over speed, even if it meant pushing deadlines.
Q16: Deadlines were powerful motivators that shaped my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd plan backwards from due dates, scheduling when to access materials and complete work. As deadlines approached, my login frequency would spike and I'd be more focused during each session. They gave structure to my week and helped me prioritize when everything felt equally urgent.
Q17: Feedback was incredibly important to me and definitely influenced my engagement. When I received constructive feedback, I'd immediately review it and apply it to my next assignment, which often meant going back to specific LMS resources. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me more willing to participate in discussions. The 20-hour average response time for feedback felt reasonable and kept me connected to the course rhythm.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my limited study time effectively. Having discussion forums as part of the structure made peer interaction feel legitimate rather than extra. The way assignments built on each other gave me a sense of progression that kept me motivated. I appreciated predictability because it allowed me to integrate coursework into my complicated life.
Q19: The peer support aspects of the course design made engagement much easier for me since feeling connected to others kept me accountable. Clear assignment rubrics helped me understand exactly what was expected, which reduced anxiety. What made it harder was when video content was the primary format since I had to find workarounds as a read-write learner. More text-based alternatives would have helped me engage more efficiently.
Q20: I think students might be exploring and trying to understand the course structure without yet feeling confident enough to submit work. They could be struggling with the material and reviewing it repeatedly without making progress. Some might be dealing with personal issues that affect their ability to complete work even when they're trying to engage. High activity without submissions might also indicate confusion about requirements or fear of failure.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience in some ways. I performed reasonably well with a Merit grade despite only logging in about twice a week because much of my learning happened offline. Students like me who download materials, study from printed notes, and connect with peers outside the LMS can be very engaged without it showing in login data. Efficiency matters more than frequency when you're balancing multiple responsibilities.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show submission patterns, deadline adherence, and participation in online discussions. They capture when you accessed materials and how you moved through the course structure. But they miss the depth of offline engagement, the quality of understanding, and the emotional journey of learning. They can't see the hours spent with printed notes, the peer study sessions, or the moments of real comprehension that happen away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's about connecting with the content intellectually and with peers socially, feeling part of a learning community. For me, engagement includes the struggle of working through difficult concepts and the satisfaction of finally grasping them. It's active participation in my own education, whether that shows up online or not.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how the peer support intervention genuinely changed my experience and helped me persist through challenges. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials on the bus or the study sessions with classmates over WhatsApp. My growth in confidence and the way I've learned to balance everything as a 29-year-old student with limited resources wouldn't be visible. Most importantly, it wouldn't show how happy and proud I feel about earning my Merit despite all the obstacles.
